network.overall_strategy: This network models adenine as a convergent prebiotic product arising from multiple literature-supported entry points that feed a shared HCN/AICN-centered reaction graph. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively as a source of reduced C1 feedstocks and formamide precursors rather than as the main adenine accumulation setting, consistent with adenine instability at high temperature. Surface environments provide the dominant productive chemistry through classical HCN oligomerization, eutectic concentration, UV-driven DAMN photoisomerization to AICN, Hill-Orgel ammonium-formate-mediated cyclization, and mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation. Biochemical/prebiotic settings are limited to assembly-level transformations already grounded in the supplied literature, avoiding unsupported nucleoside-to-adenine salvage. Ten distinct pathways are represented as different valid routes through the same connected bipartite graph, with HCN, DAMN, AICN, formamide, and formamidine serving as hubs.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases gives surface-bound formyl equivalents that are released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Included as a conservative hydrothermal feedstock-generating step consistent with Fe-S-catalyzed CO2 reduction in alkaline vent chemistry and the iron-sulfur world framework, supplying C1 precursors that can feed later surface formamide/HCN chemistry rather than claiming robust hydrothermal adenine accumulation (Wachtershauser, 1988-1992; Cody et al., 2000; Saladino et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal formamide generation from formate and ammonia

conditions.other: subseafloor rock-water interfaces; condensative amidation treated as a connecting step to literature-established formamide networks

reactions.mechanism: Formate/formic acid and ammonia undergo mineral-assisted dehydration to formamide at hydrothermal interfaces.
reactions.reasoning: This is a necessary connecting step to link hydrothermal C1 chemistry to the experimentally established formamide adenine network. It is more speculative than the downstream formamide chemistry and is included only as a geochemically plausible bridge; the literature report emphasizes formamide accumulation and hydrothermal pore concentration rather than a single canonical synthetic step (Niether et al., 2016; Saladino et al., 2001; Bada & Cleaves, 2015, noting geochemical plausibility remains debated).

reactions.name: Surface thermal decomposition of formamide to HCN

conditions.other: drying geothermal or evaporitic settings; based on formamide-HCN equilibrium and mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation systems

reactions.mechanism: Thermal dehydration/fragmentation of adsorbed formamide yields HCN, which then enters classical oligomerization chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature explicitly identifies the equilibrium HCONH2 ⇌ HCN + H2O and notes that formamide chemistry proceeds through HCN-like intermediates. This reaction is used to connect the validated formamide and HCN sub-networks while keeping mineral agents explicit for the surface setting (Saladino et al., 2001; Barks et al., 2010; Yamada et al., 1978; Slavova et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Classical HCN dimerization to iminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite concentrating surface with alkaline phosphate-buffered microenvironments
conditions.other: pH 8-9, concentrated NH4CN/HCN solution or evaporative concentration

reactions.mechanism: CN- attacks HCN in a base-catalyzed dimerization to form the HCN dimer iminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first mechanistic step of the Oró/Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel oligomerization sequence. The dimer is computationally validated though not isolated from aqueous HCN; pH 8-9 is optimal because HCN and CN- coexist. Mineral surfaces are included as concentration/organization aids in surface settings (Oro, 1960; Sanchez et al., 1967; Kikuchi et al., 2000; Sandstrom & Rahm, 2023).

reactions.name: HCN trimerization to aminomalononitrile
reactions.mechanism: Addition of a third HCN equivalent to the HCN dimer yields the highly reactive trimer aminomalononitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This follows the classical pentamerization sequence and is supported by mechanistic/computational studies and the Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel framework for HCN polymerization (Sanchez et al., 1967; Roy et al., 2007; Sandstrom & Rahm, 2025/2026).

reactions.name: HCN tetramerization to DAMN

conditions.other: pH 8-9; DAMN isolable under concentrated NH4CN chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Addition of the fourth HCN equivalent gives diaminomaleonitrile, the first stable isolable tetramer.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is a key experimentally observed HCN tetramer and pivotal stable intermediate in adenine chemistry. Its formation from concentrated HCN/NH4CN is foundational to the adenine network (Zubay & Mui, 2001; Oro, 1960; Sandstrom & Rahm, 2025/2026).

reactions.name: UV photoisomerization of DAMN to trans-DAMN
reactions.mechanism: Photons drive cis/trans isomerization of DAMN to the trans isomer (DAFN), initiating the multistep photochemical route to AICN.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris and Orgel established UV-driven DAMN photoisomerization; Michaelian and Simeonov quantified rapid AICN formation under young-Sun UV. TiO2 is included as the required surface mineral photocatalyst, consistent with UV-assisted surface photochemistry, though the core photochemistry is intrinsic to DAMN itself (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Michaelian & Simeonov, 2022; Senanayake & Idriss, 2006).

reactions.name: Photochemical conversion of trans-DAMN to AICN
reactions.mechanism: Further UV excitation rearranges trans-DAMN through ketenimine-like intermediates to 4-aminoimidazole-5-carbonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: AICN is the central hub of prebiotic purine synthesis. The trans-DAMN to AICN photorearrangement is directly supported by the Ferris-Orgel pathway and later photochemical analysis showing substantial AICN production under plausible UV flux (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Michaelian & Simeonov, 2022; Sanchez et al., 1968).

reactions.name: AICN ring closure with HCN to adenine

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surface; NH3-catalyzed bulk-water closure
conditions.other: pH 8-9, NH3 present; kinetically bottlenecked step

reactions.mechanism: AICN reacts with a fifth HCN equivalent to form the purine pyrimidine ring, yielding adenine after cyclization and tautomerization.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical final step of classical HCN pentamerization. The literature identifies it as the kinetic bottleneck, with NH3 catalysis sharply lowering the barrier. Mineral surfaces are included here as concentration aids in evaporitic pools, not as a claim of a distinct mineral-specific catalytic mechanism (Sanchez et al., 1968; Roy et al., 2007; Armas-Vazquez et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Thermal conversion of HCN-derived intermediates to formamidine

conditions.other: evaporitic concentration; formamidine detected in HCN oligomerization mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia-assisted conversion within concentrated HCN oligomerization mixtures furnishes formamidine as a one-carbon/one-nitrogen donor.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report explicitly identifies formamidine as detected in HCN oligomerization mixtures and as the alternative donor for AICN cyclization. This step is represented as a mixture-level productive node connecting classical HCN chemistry to the Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel branch chemistry (Sanchez et al., 1968; Zubay & Mui, 2001; Hill & Orgel, 2002).

reactions.name: AICN ring closure with formamidine to adenine

conditions.other: warm evaporitic or wet-dry cycling regime

reactions.mechanism: Formamidine supplies the C-2/N fragment for cyclocondensation with AICN to close the purine ring and generate adenine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the established alternative terminal branch from the AICN hub and is specifically highlighted in Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel's work and in later summaries of the AICN-centered purine network (Sanchez et al., 1968; Zubay & Mui, 2001).

reactions.name: Formation of ammonium formate from formic acid and ammonia
reactions.mechanism: Acid-base neutralization and evaporation concentrate ammonium formate as a reactive salt for subsequent thermolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Hill & Orgel used ammonium formate as the productive additive in the highest-yield one-pot prebiotic adenine route from DAMN. This step formalizes its acquisition from plausible prebiotic feedstocks in a surface evaporitic setting (Hill & Orgel, 2002; Pearce et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Thermolysis of ammonium formate to formamidine

conditions.other: 110C dry heating; in situ donor generation in Hill-Orgel regime

reactions.mechanism: Heating ammonium formate generates formamidine in situ, enabling subsequent cyclization chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report explicitly states that ammonium formate can substitute for formamidine because it converts to formamidine in situ under the Hill-Orgel conditions. This is the key bridge between the salt and the productive cyclization donor (Hill & Orgel, 2002).

reactions.name: Hill-Orgel adenine synthesis from DAMN and ammonium formate

conditions.other: 110C, one-pot route bypassing explicit UV step; ~18% yield

reactions.mechanism: Upon heating, ammonium formate generates formamidine-like reactive species in situ, allowing DAMN to cyclize through a bypass route to adenine without requiring the photochemical DAMN-to-AICN sequence as an isolated step.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best-yielding prebiotically plausible one-pot adenine synthesis in the supplied literature and must be represented explicitly. It bypasses the need to isolate the photochemical AICN route and gives ~18% adenine at 110C (Hill & Orgel, 2002).

reactions.name: Mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation to adenine
reactions.mechanism: Condensed formamide undergoes thermally driven self-catalytic decomposition and recombination via HCN-like intermediates to furnish adenine in one pot.
reactions.reasoning: Saladino and coworkers showed that heating neat formamide with mineral catalysts such as TiO2 and montmorillonite yields adenine among the canonical nucleobases. This pathway is experimentally validated and distinct from the explicit stepwise HCN route, even though mechanistically it passes through related intermediates (Saladino et al., 2001; Saladino et al., 2003; Saladino et al., 2004; Slavova et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Iron-sulfur mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation to adenine

conditions.other: 160C interfacial/mineral-rich formamide chemistry; included cautiously because adenine stability at hotter vents is poor

reactions.mechanism: Fe-S mineral surfaces catalyze formamide condensation through radical/condensation chemistry analogous to the broader formamide nucleobase network.
reactions.reasoning: Pyrite/pyrrhotite-catalyzed formamide condensation to purines is directly supported, but this node is treated cautiously as a localized mineral-assisted production step rather than evidence for sustained hydrothermal adenine accumulation, in line with the supplied warning about adenine instability at elevated hydrothermal temperatures (Saladino et al., 2008; Levy & Miller, 1998; Franiatte et al., 2008).

pathways.name: Classical Oró pentamerization route
pathways.description: Canonical HCN oligomerization in concentrated surface cyanide solutions progressing through dimer, trimer, DAMN, UV-driven AICN, then closure with HCN to adenine.

pathways.name: AICN-formamidine closure route
pathways.description: Classical HCN oligomerization to AICN followed by the Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel branch using formamidine instead of HCN for the final ring closure.

pathways.name: Hill-Orgel DAMN plus ammonium formate route
pathways.description: High-yield one-pot dry-heating route in which HCN oligomerization furnishes DAMN and surface-generated ammonium formate enables direct adenine formation.

pathways.name: Hill-Orgel via explicit in situ formamidine generation
pathways.description: Variant of the ammonium formate route made explicit by thermolysis to formamidine before productive cyclization chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface formamide then direct mineral condensation
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies formate and formamide, which are transported to surface mineral settings where neat-formamide condensation yields adenine directly.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface formamide-to-HCN classical route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal generation of formamide feeds surface decomposition to HCN, which then follows the full classical photochemical AICN route to adenine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface formamide-to-HCN formamidine branch
pathways.description: Same cross-environment handoff as P6, but the final AICN closure uses formamidine generated in the HCN mixture.

pathways.name: Surface formamide direct adenine synthesis on TiO2
pathways.description: One-pot adenine synthesis from neat formamide under surface heating on TiO2-rich mineral assemblages.

pathways.name: Surface formamide direct adenine synthesis on montmorillonite
pathways.description: One-pot adenine synthesis from neat formamide on clay-rich surfaces, representing the montmorillonite-supported branch of the same validated formamide chemistry.

pathways.name: Localized iron-sulfur formamide condensation route
pathways.description: Cautious hydrothermal/interfacial branch in which iron-sulfur minerals catalyze formamide condensation to adenine under the Saladino iron-sulfur conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature alignment with the classical Oró/Ferris-Orgel route: HCN oligomerization to DAMN, UV conversion through trans-DAMN to AICN, then ring closure to adenine. The main weakness is that productive HCN polymerization usually requires explicit concentration conditions such as eutectic freezing or evaporitic concentration, which are not encoded in the pathway summary. The final AICN + HCN step is correctly included but is kinetically difficult and would benefit from explicit ammonia/base catalysis and pH 8-9.
suggestions: Specify concentration regime for HCN (eutectic freezing or evaporitic concentration).
Annotate ammonia/base catalysis and pH 8-9 for the AICN to adenine closure step.
Include expected low yield/asphalt competition in reasoning.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a good literature-grounded branch because AICN + formamidine is explicitly supported as a canonical adenine-forming closure. The main concern is rxn_010: representing formamidine as arising directly from HCN + NH3 is too compressed and somewhat underspecified relative to the literature, where formamidine is detected in complex HCN oligomerization mixtures or generated in situ from ammonium formate. The final closure itself is strong.
suggestions: Recast formamidine formation as occurring via HCN oligomerization mixtures or via ammonium formate thermolysis.
Add explicit thermal/pH context for formamidine generation.
Cite Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1968) and Hill & Orgel (2002) for this branch.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest pathway in the network. DAMN plus ammonium formate at about 110°C is directly supported by Hill & Orgel and is one of the highest-yielding plausible adenine routes. The pathway sensibly bypasses the photochemical bottleneck and uses a validated thermal closure. It would be even stronger if the 110°C condition and in situ conversion of ammonium formate to formamidine were made explicit.
suggestions: Explicitly state 110°C thermal conditions for the DAMN + ammonium formate step.
Note that this route bypasses AICN photochemistry but remains consistent with literature.
Mention the reported ~18% adenine yield.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A credible variant of the Hill-Orgel/formamidine branch. The explicit ammonium formate to formamidine conversion is literature-consistent, but including both rxn_013 and rxn_010 makes the route somewhat redundant and less clean mechanistically. The adenine-forming closure from AICN + formamidine is good; the weakest part remains the direct HCN + NH3 to formamidine transformation.
suggestions: Prefer rxn_013 over rxn_010 as the source of formamidine.
Clarify whether the pathway proceeds through AICN or directly from DAMN under thermal conditions.
Remove redundant formamidine-generation logic.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cross-environment logic is good: hydrothermal reduced C1 chemistry feeds a surface formamide condensation route. However, the weak point is hydrothermal generation of formamide from formate and ammonia, which is not a well-established direct prebiotic step under the stated conditions. The final formamide-to-adenine step is literature-supported on minerals, but should be tied to known catalysts and temperature ranges from Saladino and coworkers.
suggestions: Replace or qualify rxn_002 as speculative unless specific literature support is provided.
Specify catalyst and temperature for rxn_015 (e.g., TiO2 or montmorillonite, 100-160°C).
Acknowledge adenine instability in prolonged hydrothermal residence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A coherent multistage route from hydrothermal C1 reduction to surface HCN chemistry. It aligns with the literature concept that hydrothermal settings may supply feedstocks while surface settings dominate adenine formation. The main weakness is still the poorly supported direct formate/ammonia to formamide step. Once HCN is available, the later chemistry is strong.
suggestions: Strengthen the feedstock transfer by citing thermophoretic accumulation or alternative formamide generation literature.
Specify concentration and pH conditions for HCN polymerization.
State that hydrothermal chemistry is feedstock-generating rather than adenine-accumulating.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route benefits from the same good environment-to-environment flow as P6, but accumulates two weak links: rxn_002 and rxn_010. The downstream AICN + formamidine closure is literature-supported, but the route would be more convincing if formamidine came from ammonium formate thermolysis rather than a direct HCN + NH3 transformation.
suggestions: Use ammonium formate thermolysis as the sole formamidine source.
Clarify the specific surface setting for HCN concentration and photochemistry.
Reduce speculative compression in early feedstock steps.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct adenine synthesis from formamide on TiO2 is well aligned with the formamide literature. As a one-step pathway it is compact and valid, but it contributes less to the broader network story because it bypasses the shared HCN/AICN hubs. It is chemically credible if the temperature window and catalyst identity are explicit.
suggestions: Make TiO2 catalyst and 100-160°C range explicit.
Note that formamide likely decomposes through HCN-like intermediates even if not shown explicitly.
Include yield expectations (<1% typical).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct adenine synthesis from formamide on montmorillonite is broadly literature-consistent. The pathway is plausible, though it is less specific than the TiO2 case because the network summary does not distinguish catalyst-specific outcomes. Good as an alternative entry point, but not as strongly connected to the AICN-centered network architecture.
suggestions: Differentiate montmorillonite-specific evidence from TiO2-specific evidence.
Specify thermal conditions and whether the medium is neat formamide.
Mention that selectivity is limited and multiple nucleobases are expected.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest pathway. While iron-sulfur mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation is supported, placing adenine production itself in a hydrothermal setting is in tension with the literature emphasizing adenine instability at elevated hydrothermal temperatures. In addition, the direct hydrothermal formamide generation step is weakly supported. This pathway should likely be treated as speculative or relocated to lower-temperature mineral settings.
suggestions: Move iron-sulfur mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation to a moderate-temperature surface or subaerial geothermal context.
Explicitly acknowledge adenine instability in hydrothermal environments.
Support rxn_016 with the specific pyrite/pyrrhotite 160°C literature if retained.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong, well-connected adenine synthesis network with good topology, clear hubs, and multiple convergent routes that align well with the supplied literature. Its biggest strengths are: correct identification of HCN, DAMN, AICN, formamide, and formamidine as central intermediates; inclusion of the classical Oró/Ferris-Orgel pathway; inclusion of the Hill-Orgel DAMN + ammonium formate route; and recognition that hydrothermal settings are better used as feedstock sources than as the main adenine accumulation environment. The network is connected and genuinely convergent, and it successfully reaches adenine through several experimentally grounded routes. The main weaknesses are chemical overcompression in two steps: hydrothermal formamide generation from formate + ammonia (rxn_002) and direct HCN + NH3 to formamidine (rxn_010). These are not as strongly established as the rest of the graph and reduce overall confidence. A further issue is that the direct formamide-to-adenine reactions are literature-supported, but their catalyst-specific distinctions and exact conditions are not sufficiently differentiated. Finally, the hydrothermal direct adenine route is less consistent with the stability literature. Overall, the network is good to very good, but falls short of approval at the stricter >=0.9 overall threshold because several key supporting steps are underspecified or somewhat speculative.
